content.title: Doctoring sales
content.summary: {'A': '这段话主要讲述了一位全球大型制药公司的销售代表Kim Schaefer在纽约的一家医疗中心给医生提供最新产品信息和免费样品时，医生开玩笑地问她有什么好处。', 'B': '这段话主要讲了一位药品代表在推销药品时所能提供的各种礼品、样品、报酬和福利，以及她可以向医生支付200美元来让他们为符合药品概况的六名患者开具处方，并且还可以提供1000美元的报酬来邀请医生参加公司的教育讲座。\n简短总结：药品代表提供各种礼品、样品、报酬和福利，以及支付医生开具处方和参加讲座的机会。', 'C': '这段话主要讲述了销售药品是一种日常的道德判断，销售人员在提供免费餐食以购买潜在客户的时间和贿赂医生开处方之间徘徊，他们工作在一个因其销售和营销行为备受批评的行业中，但却陷入了一个古老的鸡生蛋问题的中间——企业不会使用无效的策略，所以医生应该为药品营销的不断扩大的奢华负责？还是行业有责任决定边界？\n简短总结：销售药品是一种日常的道德判断，销售人员在提供免费餐食以购买潜在客户的时间和贿赂医生开处方之间徘徊，他们工作在一个备受批评的行业中，但却陷入了一个古老的鸡生蛋问题的中间。', 'D': '销售人员数量的激增和用于推广他们事业的资金量的爆炸式增长，迫使我们对药品代表和医生之间的压力、影响和关系进行密切审视。销售人员为医生提供了非常需要的信息和教育，很多情况下，他们提供的华丽宣传册、文章复印件和处方是医疗保健提供者药品教育的主要来源。由于行业对面对面销售的巨大投资，销售人员实际上已经成为一种药物或药物组的专家，这对于需要快速信息的忙碌医生来说是一个巨大的优势。', 'E': '这段话主要讲述了医药公司在推销产品时常常采取的手段，包括送餐、在温暖阳光明媚的地方开会以及赠送宣传小工具等，但这些手段是否真的有效，很难说。', 'F': '免费样品是让医生和患者对产品忠诚的最有效方式，但可能会导致医生改变处方习惯。', 'G': '总结：制药公司整体上在市场营销方面的投资超过了研发投资，而患者们则以飞涨的处方药价格为代价，支付了每一支送出的笔、每一张免费戏票和每一顿牛排晚餐；尽管制药公司有权盈利并将继续寻找新的增加销售额的方式，但随着医疗界对可接受和不可接受行为的争论不断，显然公司必须继续受到严格审查其销售和市场营销策略。'}

paragraphs.label: A
paragraphs.text: A few months ago Kim Schaefer, sales representative of a major global pharmaceutical company, walked into a medical center in New York to bring information and free samples of her company's latest products. That day she was lucky - a doctor was available to see her. 'The last rep offered me a trip to Florida. What do you have?' the physician asked. He was only half joking.

paragraphs.label: B
paragraphs.text: What was on offer that day was a pair of tickets for a New York musical. But on any given day, what Schaefer can offer is typical for today's drug rep - a car trunk full of promotional gifts and gadgets, a budget that could buy lunches and dinners for a small country, hundreds of free drug samples and the freedom to give a physician $200 to prescribe her new product to the next six patients who fit the drug's profile. And she also has a few $1,000 honoraria to offer in exchange for doctors' attendance at her company's next educational lecture.

paragraphs.label: C
paragraphs.text: Selling pharmaceuticals is a daily exercise in ethical judgment. Salespeople like Schaefer walk the line between the common practice of buying a prospect's time with a free meal, and bribing doctors to prescribe their drugs. They work in an industry highly criticized for its sales and marketing practices, but find themselves in the middle of the age-old chicken-or-egg question - businesses won't use strategies that don't work, so are doctors to blame for the escalating extravagance of pharmaceutical marketing? Or is it the industry's responsibility to decide the boundaries?

paragraphs.label: D
paragraphs.text: The explosion in the sheer number of salespeople in the field - and the amount of funding used to promote their causes - forces close examination of the pressures, influences and relationships between drug reps and doctors. Salespeople provide much-needed information and education to physicians. In many cases the glossy brochures, article reprints and prescriptions they deliver are primary sources of drug education for healthcare givers. With the huge investment the industry has placed in face-to-face selling, salespeople have essentially become specialists in one drug or group of drugs - a tremendous advantage in getting the attention of busy doctors in need of quick information.

paragraphs.label: E
paragraphs.text: But the sales push rarely stops in the office. The flashy brochures and pamphlets left by the sales reps are often followed up with meals at expensive restaurants, meetings in warm and sunny places, and an inundation of promotional gadgets. Rarely do patients watch a doctor write with a pen that isn't emblazoned with a drug's name, or see a nurse use a tablet not bearing a pharmaceutical company's logo. Millions of dollars are spent by pharmaceutical companies on promotional products like coffee mugs, shirts, umbrellas, and golf balls. Money well spent? It's hard to tell. 'I've been the recipient of golf balls from one company and I use them, but it doesn't make me prescribe their medicine,' says one doctor. 'I tend to think I'm not influenced by what they give me.'

paragraphs.label: F
paragraphs.text: Free samples of new and expensive drugs might be the single most effective way of getting doctors and patients to become loyal to a product. Salespeople hand out hundreds of dollars' worth of samples each week - $7.2 billion worth of them in one year. Though few comprehensive studies have been conducted, one by the University of Washington investigated how drug sample availability affected what physicians prescribe. A total of 131 doctors self-reported their prescribing patterns - the conclusion was that the availability of samples led them to dispense and prescribe drugs that differed from their preferred drug choice.

paragraphs.label: G
paragraphs.text: The bottom line is that pharmaceutical companies as a whole invest more in marketing than they do in research and development. And patients are the ones who pay - in the form of sky-rocketing prescription prices - for every pen that's handed out, every free theatre ticket, and every steak dinner eaten. In the end the fact remains that pharmaceutical companies have every right to make a profit and will continue to find new ways to increase sales. But as the medical world continues to grapple with what's acceptable and what's not, it is clear that companies must continue to be heavily scrutinized for their sales and marketing strategies.

groups.title: List of Headings
groups.desc:
Questions 1-7

Reading Passage 1 has seven paragraphs, A-G.
Choose the correct heading for each paragraph from the list of headings below.

list.text: Who is responsible for the increase in promotions?
An example of what doctors expect from drug companies
The positive side of drugs promotion
Who really pays for doctors' free gifts?

questions.analysis: 这部分文章描述了医生对制药公司代表所提供的好处（如旅行、音乐剧票等）的期望。这与标题v: An example of what doctors expect from drug companies（医生对制药公司期望的一个例子）最直接对应。通过识别文章中的关键表达和理解段落主旨，学生可以正确选择这个标题。

central_sentences.sentence: The last rep offered me a trip to Florida. What do you have?
central_sentences.translation: 上一位销售代表给我提供了去佛罗里达的旅行，你有什么？

target_sen.target_sen: the physician asked.

questions.analysis: Section B详细描述了药品销售代表提供的各种礼物和经济激励。关键词“promotional gifts and gadgets”和“$200 to prescribe her new product”以及“$1,000 honoraria”直接对应了小标题vi: Gifts include financial incentives。因此，Section B的最佳小标题是vi。

central_sentences.sentence: what Schaefer can offer is typical for today's drugs rep - a car trunk full of promotional gifts and gadgets, a budget that could buy lunches and dinners for a small country, hundreds of free drug samples and the freedom to give a physician $200 to prescribe her new product to the next six patients who fit the drug's profile. And she also has a few $1,000 honoraria to offer in exchange for doctors' attendance at her company's next educational lecture
central_sentences.translation: Schaefer能够提供的是当今药品代表的典型做法——满后备箱的促销礼品和小玩意，足以为一个小国家提供午餐和晚餐的预算，数百份免费药品样本，以及给医生200美元让他们为符合药品特征的下六位病人开出新药的自由。她还可以提供几笔1000美元的酬金，以换取医生参加公司下一次的教育讲座。

target_sen.target_sen: But on any given day, what Schaefer can offer is typical for today's drugs rep - a car trunk full of promotional gifts and gadgets, a budget that could buy lunches and dinners for a small country, hundreds of free drug samples and the freedom to give a physician $200 to prescribe her new product to the next six patients who fit the drug's profile.

But on any given day, what Schaefer can offer is typical for today's drugs rep - a car trunk full of promotional gifts and gadgets, a budget that could buy lunches and dinners for a small country, hundreds of free drug samples and the freedom to give a physician $200 to prescribe her new product to the next six patients who fit the drug's profile.
And she also has a few $1,000 honoraria to offer in exchange for doctors' attendance at her company's next educational lecture.

questions.analysis: 这段话讨论了药品销售和促销策略的责任归属问题，即到底是医生的需求推动了促销策略的升级，还是行业本身应对其促销策略负责。这与小标题 iii: Who is responsible for the increase in promotions? 完全对应。段落中的“responsibility”和“escalating extravagance of pharmaceutical marketing”与题目中的“responsible”和“increase in promotions”是同义替换词。

central_sentences.sentence: Salespeople like Schaefer walk the line between the common practice of buying a prospect's time with a free meal, and bribing doctors to prescribe their drugs. They work in an industry highly criticized for its sales and marketing practices, but find themselves in the middle of the age-old chicken-or-egg question - businesses won't use strategies that don't work, so are doctors to blame for the escalating extravagance of pharmaceutical marketing? Or is it the industry's responsibility to decide the boundaries?
central_sentences.translation: 像谢佛这样的销售人员游走在用免费餐点换取潜在客户时间的常见做法，和贿赂医生开具他们药品处方之间的灰色地带。他们所在的行业因其销售和营销手法备受批评，但他们发现自己陷入了一个古老的鸡生蛋还是蛋生鸡的问题——企业不会使用无效的策略，那么是否应该责怪医生导致了制药营销的过度奢侈？还是应该由行业自己来决定界限？

groups.desc:
Questions 8-13

Do the following statements agree with the claims of the writer in Reading Passage 1?
Choose
YES if the statement agrees with the claims of the writer,
NO if the statement contradicts the claims of the writer,
NOT GIVEN if it is impossible to say what the writer thinks about this.

questions.title: Sales representatives like Kim Schaefer work to a very limited budget.
questions.analysis: 题干中的“limited budget”意思是“有限的预算”，而原文中提到的“a budget that could buy lunches and dinners for a small country”意思是“一个可以为一个小国家买午餐和晚餐的预算”，这显然是一个非常大的预算。因此，题干中的信息与原文内容相反，所以正确答案是NO。

central_sentences.sentence: What was on offer that day was a pair of tickets for a New York musical. But on any given day, what Schaefer can offer is typical for today's drugs rep - a car trunk full of promotional gifts and gadgets, a budget that could buy lunches and dinners for a small country, hundreds of free drug samples and the freedom to give a physician $200 to prescribe her new product to the next six patients who fit the drug's profile.
central_sentences.translation: 那天提供的是一对纽约音乐剧门票。但在任何一天，Schaefer能提供的都是当今药品代表的典型做法——一车后备箱的促销礼品和小玩意，一个可以为小国家购买午餐和晚餐的预算，数百份免费药品样品，以及可以给医生200美元让他们为接下来六个符合药品特征的患者开具新产品处方的自由。

syn_ary.synonym: a budget that could buy lunches and dinners for a small country

target_sen.target_sen: But on any given day, what Schaefer can offer is typical for today's drugs rep - a car trunk full of promotional gifts and gadgets, a budget that could buy lunches and dinners for a small country, hundreds of free drug samples and the freedom to give a physician $200 to prescribe her new product to the next six patients who fit the drug's profile.

questions.title: Kim Schaefer's marketing technique may be open to criticism on moral grounds.
questions.analysis: 题干中的 "marketing technique" 对应原文中的 "Selling pharmaceuticals"，"criticism on moral grounds" 对应原文中的 "ethical judgement" 和 "walk the line between... buying a prospect's time with a free meal, and bribing doctors to prescribe their drugs"。原文明确指出，销售药品涉及伦理判断，并且销售人员在购买时间和贿赂医生之间徘徊，这表明了她的营销技术在道德上可能会受到批评。因此，答案是 YES。

central_sentences.sentence: Selling pharmaceuticals is a daily exercise in ethical judgement. Salespeople like Schaefer walk the line between the common practice of buying a prospect's time with a free meal, and bribing doctors to prescribe their drugs.
central_sentences.translation: 销售药品是每天都要进行的伦理判断练习。像Schaefer这样的销售人员在用免费餐点换取客户时间这种常见做法和贿赂医生开具他们的药品之间走钢丝。

questions.analysis: 题干中的信息“of little use”与原文中的“much-needed”和“primary sources”形成对比。原文明确指出药品销售代表提供的信息对医生是非常需要的，并且是医生药物教育的主要来源。因此，题干中的陈述是错误的，正确答案是“NO”。

content.title: Do literate women make better mothers?

paragraphs.label: A
paragraphs.text: Children in developing countries are healthier and more likely to survive past the age of five when their mothers can read and write. Experts in public health accepted this idea decades ago, but until now no one has been able to show that a woman's ability to read in itself improves her children's chances of survival.

paragraphs.label: B
paragraphs.text: Most literate women learnt to read in primary school, and the fact that a woman has had an education may simply indicate her family's wealth or that it values its children more highly. Now a long-term study carried out in Nicaragua has eliminated these factors by showing that teaching reading to poor adult women, who would otherwise have remained illiterate, has a direct effect on their children's health and survival.

paragraphs.label: C
paragraphs.text: In 1979, the government of Nicaragua established a number of social programmes, including a National Literacy Crusade. By 1985, about 300,000 illiterate adults from all over the country, many of whom had never attended primary school, had learnt how to read, write and use numbers.

paragraphs.label: D
paragraphs.text: During this period, researchers from the Liverpool School of Tropical Medicine, the Central American Institute of Health in Nicaragua, the National Autonomous University of Nicaragua and the Costa Rican Institute of Health interviewed nearly 3,000 women, some of whom had learnt to read as children, some during the literacy crusade and some who had never learnt at all. The women were asked how many children they had given birth to and how many of them had died in infancy. The research teams also examined the surviving children to find out how well-nourished they were.

paragraphs.label: E
paragraphs.text: The investigators' findings were striking. In the late 1970s, the infant mortality rate for the children of illiterate mothers was around 110 deaths per thousand live births. At this point in their lives, those mothers who later went on to learn to read had a similar level of child mortality (105/1000). For women educated in primary school, however, the infant mortality rate was significantly lower, at 80 per thousand.

paragraphs.label: F
paragraphs.text: In 1985, after the National Literacy Crusade had ended, the infant mortality figures for those who remained illiterate and for those educated in primary school remained more or less unchanged. For those women who learnt to read through the campaign, the infant mortality rate was 84 per thousand, an impressive 21 points lower than for those women who were still illiterate. The children of the newly-literate mothers were also better nourished than those of women who could not read.

paragraphs.label: G
paragraphs.text: Why are the children of literate mothers better off? According to Peter Sandiford of the Liverpool School of Tropical Medicine, no one knows for certain. Child health was not on the curriculum during the women's lessons, so he and his colleagues are looking at other factors. They are working with the same group of 3,000 women, to try to find out whether reading mothers make better use of hospitals and clinics, opt for smaller families, exert more control at home, learn modern child care techniques more quickly, or whether they merely have more respect for themselves and their children.

paragraphs.label: H
paragraphs.text: The Nicaraguan study may have important implications for governments and aid agencies that need to know where to direct their resources. Sandiford says that there is increasing evidence that female education, at any age, is 'an important health intervention in its own right'. The results of the study lend support to the World Bank's recommendation that education budgets in developing countries should be increased, not just to help their economies, but also to improve child health.

paragraphs.label: I
paragraphs.text: 'We've known for a long time that maternal education is important,' says John Cleland of the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine. 'But we thought that even if we started educating girls today, we'd have to wait a generation for the pay-off. The Nicaraguan study suggests we may be able to bypass that.'

paragraphs.label: J
paragraphs.text: Cleland warns that the Nicaraguan crusade was special in many ways, and similar campaigns elsewhere might not work as well. It is notoriously difficult to teach adults skills that do not have an immediate impact on their everyday lives, and many literacy campaigns in other countries have been much less successful. 'The crusade was part of a larger effort to bring a better life to the people,' says Cleland. Replicating these conditions in other countries will be a major challenge for development workers.

content.title: Passage 3

paragraphs.label: A
paragraphs.text: Bullying can take a variety of forms, from the verbal—being taunted or called hurtful names—to the physical—being kicked or shoved—as well as indirect forms, such as being excluded from social groups. A survey I conducted with Irene Whitney found that in British primary schools up to a quarter of pupils reported experience of bullying, which in about one in ten cases was persistent. There was less bullying in secondary schools, with about one in twenty-five suffering persistent bullying, but these cases may be particularly recalcitrant.

paragraphs.label: B
paragraphs.text: Bullying is clearly unpleasant, and can make the child experiencing it feel unworthy and depressed. In extreme cases it can even lead to suicide, though this is thankfully rare. Victimised pupils are more likely to experience difficulties with interpersonal relationships as adults, while children who persistently bully are more likely to grow up to be physically violent, and convicted of anti-social offences.

paragraphs.label: C
paragraphs.text: Until recently, not much was known about the topic, and little help was available to teachers to deal with bullying. Perhaps as a consequence, schools would often deny the problem. 'There is no bullying at this school' has been a common refrain, almost certainly untrue. Fortunately more schools are now saying: 'There is not much bullying here, but when it occurs we have a clear policy for dealing with it.'

paragraphs.label: D
paragraphs.text: Three factors are involved in this change. First is an awareness of the severity of the problem. Second, a number of resources to help tackle bullying have become available in Britain. For example, the Scottish Council for Research in Education produced a package of materials, Action Against Bullying, circulated to all schools in England and Wales as well as in Scotland in summer 1992, with a second pack, Supporting Schools Against Bullying, produced the following year. In Ireland, Guidelines on Countering Bullying Behaviour in Post-Primary Schools was published in 1993. Third, there is evidence that these materials work, and that schools can achieve something. This comes from carefully conducted 'before and after' evaluations of interventions in schools, monitored by a research team. In Norway, after an intervention campaign was introduced nationally, an evaluation of forty-two schools suggested that, over a two-year period, bullying was halved. The Sheffield investigation, which involved sixteen primary schools and seven secondary schools, found that most schools succeeded in reducing bullying.

paragraphs.label: E
paragraphs.text:
Evidence suggests that a key step is to develop a policy on bullying, saying clearly what is meant by bullying, and giving explicit guidelines on what will be done if it occurs, what records will be kept, who will be informed, what sanctions will be employed. The policy should be developed through consultation, over a period of time—not just imposed from the head teacher's office! Pupils, parents and staff should feel they have been involved in the policy, which needs to be disseminated and implemented effectively.
Other actions can be taken to back up the policy. There are ways of dealing with the topic through the curriculum, using video, drama and literature. These are useful for raising awareness, and can best be tied in to early phases of development, while the school is starting to discuss the issue of bullying. They are also useful in renewing the policy for new pupils, or revising it in the light of experience. But curriculum work alone may only have short-term effects; it should be an addition to policy work, not a substitute.
There are also ways of working with individual pupils, or in small groups. Assertiveness training for pupils who are liable to be victims is worthwhile, and certain approaches to group bullying such as 'no blame', can be useful in changing the behaviour of bullying pupils without confronting them directly, although other sanctions may be needed for those who continue with persistent bullying.
Work in the playground is important, too. One helpful step is to train lunchtime supervisors to distinguish bullying from playful fighting, and help them break up conflicts. Another possibility is to improve the playground environment, so that pupils are less likely to be led into bullying from boredom or frustration.

paragraphs.label: F
paragraphs.text: With these developments, schools can expect that at least the most serious kinds of bullying can largely be prevented. The more effort put in and the wider the whole school involvement, the more substantial the results are likely to be. The reduction in bullying—and the consequent improvement in pupil happiness—is surely a worthwhile objective.

Evidence suggests that a key step is to develop a policy on bullying, saying clearly what is meant by bullying, and giving explicit guidelines on what will be done if it occurs, what records will be kept, who will be informed, what sanctions will be employed.
These are useful for raising awareness, and can best be tied in to early phases of development, while the school is starting to discuss the issue of bullying.

Assertiveness training for pupils who are liable to be victims is worthwhile, and certain approaches to group bullying such as 'no blame', can be useful in changing the behaviour of bullying pupils without confronting them directly, although other sanctions may be needed for those who continue with persistent bullying.
One helpful step is to train lunchtime supervisors to distinguish bullying from playful fighting, and help them break up conflicts.
Another possibility is to improve the playground environment, so that pupils are less likely to be led into bullying from boredom or frustration.

questions.title: Which of the following is the most suitable title for Reading Passage 3?

options.text: Bullying: are the media to blame?
Bullying: the link with academic failure
Bullying: from crisis management to prevention

questions.analysis: 文章主要讨论了校园欺凌的各种形式、影响以及学校如何应对和预防欺凌行为。文章提到了一些具体的措施和政策，从最初的危机管理到现在的预防措施。因此，选项D "Bullying: from crisis management to prevention" 是最合适的标题。

central_sentences.sentence: Until recently, not much was known about the topic, and little help was available to teachers to deal with bullying. Perhaps as a consequence, schools would often deny the problem. 'There is no bullying at this school' has been a common refrain, almost certainly untrue. Fortunately more schools are now saying: 'There is not much bullying here, but when it occurs we have a clear policy for dealing with it.'
central_sentences.translation: 直到最近，人们对这个话题知之甚少，教师也几乎没有可用的资源来处理欺凌问题。可能正是因为这个原因，学校通常会否认存在这个问题。"我们学校没有欺凌"曾是一句常见的说辞，但几乎肯定是不真实的。幸运的是，现在越来越多的学校开始说："这里欺凌不多，但一旦发生，我们有明确的政策来处理。"
